name: Coalition Diversification
consequences: Pulling this lever expands the coalition beyond civil society to include private security firms, academia, and industry partners, increasing influence but also risking conflicting interests and dilution of focus. The broader coalition can mobilize diverse expertise, yet it may slow consensus and complicate messaging.
options: Invite private security firms to join the coalition, leveraging their threat intelligence to strengthen resilience arguments.
Add academic researchers from multiple universities to the coalition, broadening technical expertise and enhancing credibility.
Invite industry partners from non‑identity sectors to the coalition, bringing fresh procurement perspectives and potential funding sources.
review: Expanding coalition breadth risks diluting advocacy focus, yet options ignore how divergent stakeholder priorities could fragment messaging and slow decision‑making.

name: Regulatory Framing
consequences: Pulling this lever reframes platform dependency as a national security risk, potentially accelerating procurement changes but may trigger pushback from incumbents citing cost and complexity. The security framing can unlock policy levers, yet it may also provoke cost‑justification arguments that stall reforms.
options: Position platform neutrality as a national security imperative, linking it to Denmark’s cyber‑defense strategy for national resilience.
Frame fallback authentication as a resilience requirement under eIDAS, emphasizing continuity of essential public services.
Advocate for procurement clauses that mandate platform‑neutral fallback as a compliance metric, ensuring future contracts embed resilience.
review: Framing platform neutrality as a security priority clashes with incumbents’ cost‑justification arguments, yet options ignore how cost concerns could stall procurement changes.

name: Technical Demonstrator Scope
consequences: Pulling this lever tightens demonstrator scope to focus on core proof of concept, reducing risk of scope creep but limiting stakeholder engagement opportunities. A focused demo can prove feasibility quickly, yet it may not showcase the breadth needed for stakeholder buy‑in.
options: Limit the demonstrator to a single FIDO2/WebAuthn mobile prototype, concentrating resources on core proof of concept.
Expand the demonstrator to include a browser‑based fallback token, illustrating platform‑neutral access across devices for broader stakeholder validation.
Introduce a hybrid demonstrator combining mobile and hardware token, showcasing versatility and cross‑platform resilience for comprehensive stakeholder confidence.
review: Focusing on depth risks missing breadth of stakeholder validation, yet options ignore the necessity of usability testing with end‑users.

name: Funding Allocation
consequences: Pulling this lever reallocates budget to prioritize procurement engagement, potentially reducing technical depth but increasing policy influence. The reallocation can accelerate policy traction, yet it may compromise the quality of technical evidence presented.
options: Shift 1.5 million DKK from phase two to phase three procurement lobbying, ensuring timely policy engagement.
Allocate 1.0 million DKK to external legal counsel for procurement clause drafting, securing robust contractual language.
Reserve 0.5 million DKK for contingency to address unforeseen regulatory delays, preserving project continuity and stakeholder confidence.
review: Reallocating funds to procurement risks compromising demonstrator quality, yet options overlook how reduced technical depth could weaken evidence credibility.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Pulling this lever aligns Danish advocacy with EU Digital Identity Wallet, potentially amplifying influence but may dilute national focus and create regulatory friction. EU alignment can open broader channels, yet it may clash with Denmark’s procurement timelines.
options: Submit a technical white paper to the EU Digital Identity Working Group, positioning Denmark as a thought leader.
Participate in the EU interoperability testbed, demonstrating platform‑neutral fallback capabilities to a broader audience for cross‑border validation.
Lobby for EU policy language that mandates platform neutrality in cross‑border identity flows, ensuring long‑term resilience.
review: Aligning with EU standards may conflict with Denmark’s procurement schedule, yet options overlook how EU timelines could delay national policy adoption.

name: Stakeholder Engagement Timing
consequences: Pulling this lever accelerates early engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, potentially securing faster feedback but risking premature commitment to technical details. Early dialogue can shape policy, yet it may lock in assumptions before consensus is reached.
options: Initiate formal dialogue with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen in month two, securing early policy feedback before technical plans finalize.
Schedule a joint workshop with the MitID operator in month four, aligning technical feasibility with operational realities.
Conduct a stakeholder mapping exercise in month one to identify key decision‑makers and potential allies early.
review: Early engagement risks locking in technical assumptions before policy consensus, yet options overlook how premature commitments could constrain later flexibility.

name: Platform Integrity Redundancy
consequences: Expanding the set of integrity signals beyond Apple and Google increases resilience but also raises certification complexity and may fragment the trust model. A more diverse vendor ecosystem can slow procurement cycles and require additional compliance checks.
options: Deploy a cross‑platform integrity verification service that aggregates device attestation from open‑source frameworks, enabling fallback authentication without relying on proprietary APIs.
Mandate that all future mobile OS releases include a standardized, open attestation protocol, forcing vendors to expose integrity data to third parties.
Create a community‑driven attestation registry that allows independent auditors to validate device integrity, reducing dependence on platform‑controlled signals.
review: Tension between expanding integrity sources and maintaining a unified trust model; options overlook the risk of diluting security guarantees by introducing unverified third‑party attestation providers.

name: Fallback Authentication Architecture
consequences: Establishing a fallback authentication path reduces platform lock‑in but introduces additional authentication steps that may affect user experience and increase operational overhead for service providers. Fallback mechanisms may require new credential storage and revocation processes, potentially exposing new attack vectors.
options: Design a browser‑based WebAuthn fallback that leverages hardware security keys, allowing users to authenticate without a mobile OS.
Implement a token‑based fallback using NFC‑enabled smart cards distributed to high‑risk public service users.
Integrate a cloud‑based biometric verification service that operates independently of mobile platforms, providing an alternative credential channel.
review: Tension between ensuring fallback availability and preserving usability; options fail to address the cost and logistical burden of distributing hardware tokens at scale.

name: Procurement Language Enforcement
consequences: Embedding platform‑neutral clauses in procurement documents forces vendors to design for multiple ecosystems, potentially increasing development costs but reducing long‑term dependency. Vendors may delay delivery or seek higher prices to cover added complexity, impacting project timelines.
options: Draft procurement specifications that require explicit support for open attestation protocols and exclude mandatory use of Apple/Google APIs.
Introduce a penalty clause for vendors that fail to provide platform‑neutral fallback options within the first two years of deployment.
Offer a procurement incentive program that rewards vendors for early adoption of multi‑platform authentication frameworks.
review: Tension between vendor compliance and cost escalation; options ignore the possibility that vendors may comply superficially to avoid penalties without delivering true platform neutrality.

name: Supplier Concentration Mitigation
consequences: Reducing supplier concentration spreads risk but may fragment the ecosystem, leading to interoperability challenges and higher integration effort for public services. Fragmented solutions could increase maintenance costs and slow innovation cycles.
options: Establish a consortium of domestic technology firms to co‑develop a shared authentication middleware, reducing reliance on foreign vendors.
Require that all new identity solutions undergo a supplier diversity audit before approval, ensuring no single vendor dominates critical components.
Create a public procurement sandbox that allows multiple small vendors to test interoperability, encouraging competition and reducing concentration.
review: Core tension between diversification and ecosystem coherence; options overlook the difficulty of coordinating standards across multiple small vendors.

name: Resilience Governance
consequences: Instituting governance structures focused on resilience formalizes risk management but may add bureaucratic layers that slow decision‑making. Increased oversight could delay procurement cycles and reduce agility in responding to emerging threats.
options: Form a resilience oversight board comprising government, academia, and industry to review all identity‑related procurement decisions.
Implement a mandatory risk assessment protocol for every new authentication technology, with public reporting of findings.
Introduce a rapid‑response task force that can bypass standard procurement when a critical platform failure threatens national services.
review: Tension between oversight and agility; options fail to consider the potential for governance fatigue among stakeholders.

name: Digital Sovereignty Advocacy
consequences: Advocating for digital sovereignty raises public awareness and political pressure but may polarize stakeholders and provoke defensive postures from incumbent vendors. Heightened political scrutiny could lead to stricter regulatory scrutiny and potential delays in procurement approvals.
options: Launch a public campaign highlighting the risks of platform dependency, targeting policymakers and the media to build support for platform‑neutral policies.
Organize a series of workshops with civil society, academia, and industry to develop a shared vision of sovereign digital identity.
Publish a white paper that quantifies the economic impact of platform lock‑in, providing evidence to influence legislative debate.
review: Tension between advocacy visibility and stakeholder resistance; options miss the need to align advocacy messaging with existing procurement timelines.

name: Fallback Authentication Path Engineering
consequences: Pulling this lever leads to the design of alternative authentication flows that bypass platform integrity signals, creating a technical blueprint for fallback that can be integrated into AltID. It may increase integration complexity and require additional certification steps, potentially exposing new attack surfaces if not properly secured.
options: Develop a hardware token-based fallback that uses a secure element on the device, enabling authentication without OS-level integrity checks.
Implement a browser-based WebAuthn flow that relies on a trusted platform module in the device's firmware, decoupling from mobile OS distribution.
Create a peer-to-peer authentication protocol that uses device-to-device cryptographic handshake, eliminating reliance on centralized platform services.
review: Core tension: balancing fallback security with integration complexity; options overlook the need for a formal certification pathway that aligns with eIDAS requirements.

name: Platform-Neutral Distribution Strategy
consequences: Pulling this lever explores alternative app distribution channels that are not controlled by Apple or Google, reducing dependency on commercial app stores. It may increase user onboarding friction and require new trust mechanisms for app authenticity.
options: Establish a secure, government-hosted app repository that delivers signed binaries directly to users, bypassing commercial app stores.
Leverage open-source package managers on Linux-based mobile platforms to distribute identity apps, ensuring transparency and community vetting.
Introduce a QR-code based installation workflow that uses a trusted QR scanner app, allowing users to install identity apps from a verified source.
review: Core tension: reducing store dependence versus user trust; options ignore the challenge of convincing users to install apps outside familiar ecosystems.

name: Stakeholder Influence Cadence
consequences: Pulling this lever sets a structured engagement schedule with key stakeholders, accelerating policy approvals. It may create perception of overreach and trigger pushback from incumbents.
options: Schedule quarterly roundtable sessions with digital policy committees, privacy advocates, and industry representatives to co-create procurement language.
Deploy a rapid-response task force that meets bi-weekly to address emerging regulatory concerns and adjust advocacy messaging.
Launch a public consultation portal that aggregates stakeholder comments in real-time, feeding into a dynamic policy draft that evolves with feedback.
review: Core tension: speed of influence versus stakeholder fatigue; options fail to account for the risk of stakeholder disengagement over prolonged engagement cycles.

name: Technical Demonstrator Scope Optimization
consequences: Pulling this lever refines demonstrator focus to essential features, ensuring deliverables remain realistic. It reduces development time and resource consumption but may limit the breadth of evidence presented to policymakers, potentially weakening advocacy impact.
options: Concentrate the mobile demonstrator on a single FIDO2/WebAuthn credential flow, omitting advanced biometric features to streamline implementation.
Limit the browser fallback demonstrator to a proof-of-concept that uses a hardware token, excluding full integration with existing identity providers.
Adopt a modular architecture that allows incremental addition of features, but commits only to core authentication logic for the initial release.
review: Core tension: scope reduction versus evidence depth; options overlook the need to demonstrate interoperability with live public-sector identity services.

name: Procurement Requirement Framing
consequences: Pulling this lever shapes procurement documents to embed platform-neutral and fallback criteria, increasing likelihood that future contracts include these requirements. It may raise procurement costs and extend tender timelines due to added complexity.
options: Draft a procurement specification that mandates inclusion of a certified fallback authentication module, with clear compliance checkpoints for platform neutrality.
Propose a modular procurement approach that separates core identity services from platform-dependent components, allowing independent evaluation of each module.
Introduce a performance-based procurement clause that rewards vendors for achieving platform-neutral deployment metrics, incentivizing broader compatibility.
review: Core tension: procurement rigor versus vendor flexibility; options ignore the risk that stringent requirements may deter qualified suppliers.

name: European Digital Identity Policy Engagement
consequences: Pulling this lever aligns Danish advocacy with EU-level standards, raising visibility of platform-neutral goals. It may dilute focus on national procurement if resources are diverted to EU forums.
options: Submit a formal position paper to the European Digital Identity Working Group, highlighting the need for fallback authentication in national identity wallets.
Organize a cross-country workshop with EU member states to share technical findings and build a coalition for platform-neutral language in EU documents.
Engage EU policy makers through targeted briefings that link Danish fallback research to broader European resilience objectives, framing it as a shared security imperative.
review: Core tension: national versus EU focus; options fail to address how EU engagement might shift resources away from domestic procurement efforts.
